content:
# Acts Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 237-238 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_010_page_001.jpg, ACT_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1** In Caesarea there was a man named Cornelius, a centurion in the regiment known as the Italian Cohort. **2** He was a devout man who feared God, as did his entire household. He gave generously to those in need and prayed to God constantly. **3** One afternoon, around three o'clock, he had a vision. He clearly saw an angel of God come to him and say, "Cornelius!"

**4** He stared at the angel, terrified. "What is it, Lord?" he asked.

The angel said to him, "Your prayers and your acts of charity have risen before God as an offering he remembers. **5** Now send men to Joppa and bring back a man named Simon, who is called Peter. **6** He is staying with Simon the tanner, whose house is by the sea."

**7** As soon as the angel who spoke to him had gone, Cornelius called two of his household servants and a devout soldier from among his attendants. **8** He told them everything and sent them off to Joppa.

**9** The next day, while they were still on the road and approaching the city, Peter went up on the rooftop to pray around noon. **10** He became hungry and wanted something to eat. While the meal was being prepared, he fell into a trance. **11** He saw the sky torn open and something like a great sheet being lowered to the earth by its four corners. **12** In it were all kinds of four-footed animals and reptiles of the earth and birds of the sky. **13** Then a voice said to him, "Get up, Peter. Kill and eat."

**14** "Absolutely not, Lord!" Peter replied. "I have never eaten anything impure or unclean."

**15** The voice spoke to him a second time: "What God has made clean, you must not call impure." **16** This happened three times, and then the sheet was taken back up into the sky.

**17** While Peter was completely puzzled about what the vision could mean, the men sent by Cornelius had found their way to Simon's house and were standing at the gate. **18** They called out, asking whether Simon who was called Peter was staying there.

**19** While Peter was still turning the vision over in his mind, the Spirit said to him, "Look — three men are here looking for you. **20** Get up, go downstairs, and go with them without any hesitation, because I have sent them."

**21** So Peter went down to the men and said, "I am the one you are looking for. Why have you come?"

**22** They answered, "Cornelius, a centurion — a man who is righteous and fears God, respected by the whole Jewish nation — was directed by a holy angel to send for you, to bring you to his house, and to hear what you have to say." **23** So Peter invited them in and gave them lodging.

The next day he set out with them, and some of the believers from Joppa went along. **24** The following day they arrived in Caesarea, where Cornelius was waiting for them. He had called together his relatives and closest friends. **25** As Peter entered, Cornelius met him and fell at his feet to worship him. **26** But Peter pulled him up. "Stand up," he said. "I am only a man myself."

**27** Talking with him as they went inside, Peter found a large gathering of people. **28** He said to them, "You are well aware that it is against our law for a Jewish man to associate with or visit a Gentile. But God has shown me that I should never call any person impure or unclean. **29** That is why I came without raising any objection when I was sent for. So let me ask: why did you send for me?"

**30** Cornelius answered, "Four days ago, at this very hour — three in the afternoon — I was praying in my house when suddenly a man in dazzling clothes stood before me. **31** He said, 'Cornelius, your prayer has been heard and your acts of charity have been remembered before God. **32** So send to Joppa and ask for Simon who is called Peter. He is a guest in the house of Simon the tanner, by the sea.' **33** So I sent for you immediately, and you have been kind enough to come. Now then, we are all here in the presence of God to hear everything the Lord has commanded you to tell us."

**34** Then Peter began to speak: "Now I truly understand that God does not show favoritism, **35** but in every nation, whoever fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him. **36** He sent his word to the people of Israel, announcing the good news of peace through Jesus Christ — who is Lord of all. **37** You know what has happened throughout Judea, beginning in Galilee after the baptism that John proclaimed: **38** how God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power, and how he went around doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil, because God was with him.

**39** "We are witnesses of everything he did in the land of the Jews and in Jerusalem. They killed him by hanging him on a cross, **40** but God raised him on the third day and made him visible — **41** not to everyone, but to us, witnesses chosen by God beforehand, who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead. **42** He commanded us to proclaim to the people and to testify that he is the one God has appointed as judge of the living and the dead. **43** All the prophets testify about him: everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name."

**44** While Peter was still speaking these words, the Holy Spirit fell on everyone who was listening to the message. **45** The Jewish believers who had come with Peter were stunned — because the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out even on the Gentiles. **46** For they heard them speaking in tongues and praising God.

Then Peter said, **47** "Can anyone withhold the water for baptizing these people? They have received the Holy Spirit just as we did." **48** So he ordered that they be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they asked Peter to stay with them for a few days.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Verse 2 — "feared God" (phoboumenos ton theon):** Sinaiticus preserves the standard participial form. "God-fearer" was a technical term for Gentiles who worshipped Israel's God and followed Jewish ethical standards but had not undergone full conversion. Cornelius occupied a liminal space — devoted but outside the covenant. I render it plainly as "feared God" with the household detail, letting the narrative itself reveal the theological weight of what is about to happen.

2. **Verse 4 — "as an offering he remembers" (eis mnemosynon):** The Greek mnemosynon carries overtones of the memorial portion of a grain offering (Leviticus 2:2). Cornelius's prayers and charity have ascended like temple sacrifice — a stunning statement about a Gentile, rendered here to capture the sacrificial resonance without over-explaining it.

3. **Verse 14 — "Absolutely not, Lord!" (medamos kyrie):** Sinaiticus reads medamos rather than the more common medamos found in later manuscripts. Peter's refusal is emphatic and visceral — this is not polite demurral but horrified resistance. "Absolutely not" captures the force better than the traditional "Not so" or "By no means."

4. **Verse 15 — "What God has made clean, you must not call impure" (ha ho theos ekatharisen, sy me koinou):** The pronoun sy is emphatic — "YOU must not." God has acted; Peter has no authority to reverse the verdict. This is the theological hinge of the chapter, and I preserve the emphatic "you" in the rendering.

5. **Verse 28 — "against our law" (athemiton):** The Greek athemiton means "unlawful" or "forbidden" but does not specifically refer to Torah legislation — it refers to custom and social convention. I render it "against our law" in Peter's voice because that is how he would have experienced it, while the narrative arc itself demonstrates that God is overturning this social boundary.

6. **Verse 34 — "God does not show favoritism" (ouk estin prosopolemptes ho theos):** Literally "God is not a face-receiver" — one who judges by outward appearance or status. This is Peter's breakthrough moment. The dynamic rendering "does not show favoritism" captures the relational force: God does not privilege one people over another.

7. **Verse 36 — "who is Lord of all" (houtos estin panton kyrios):** This parenthetical declaration is theologically explosive in context. Peter is telling Gentiles that the Jewish Messiah is Lord of all people — not just Israel. I set it off with an em dash to give it the weight of a climactic aside.

8. **Verse 44 — "the Holy Spirit fell on everyone" (epepesen to pneuma to hagion):** The verb epepesen (fell upon) conveys suddenness and divine initiative. The Spirit does not trickle or seep — it falls. Peter is still mid-sentence. God does not wait for Peter to finish his sermon or issue an altar call. This is divine interruption, and the abruptness in the text is preserved in the translation.

9. **Verse 45 — "were stunned" (exestesan):** Literally "were beside themselves." The Jewish believers are not merely surprised — they are thrown into existential disorientation. Everything they assumed about who God accepts has just been overturned before their eyes. "Stunned" carries the force of being stopped in one's tracks.

10. **Sinaiticus textual note — Verse 19:** Sinaiticus reads "three men" (treis) where some manuscripts read "two men." This is consistent with 10:7, where Cornelius sends two servants and one soldier. I follow the Sinaiticus reading.

-- PNEUMA
